title: The Intervention
text: The DAWA interventions are grounded in the WHO practical toolkit for antimicrobial stewardship in low- and middle-income country health facilities, and in guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA).

title: The Intervention
text:
ZanEMR-AS — Digital Clinical Decision Support
ZanEMR is the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record system developed and deployed by the Zanzibar Ministry of Health across public dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands. ZanEMR-AS (ZanEMR — Antibiotic Stewardship) is a modified version of ZanEMR enhanced with antibiotic stewardship features, used in Intervention arms 1 and 2.
ZanEMR-AS incorporates five core features:

1. Clinical Decision Support
A pop-up appears at the point of care when the clinician selects certain diagnoses, or when specific diagnosis–treatment combinations are entered. The guidance takes the form of a concise rule-based algorithm (2–3 steps) with checkboxes, helping the clinician decide whether an antibiotic is indicated and, if so, which one.
The algorithms are developed from the Zanzibar and Tanzania Standard Treatment Guidelines, the WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI), the WHO IMPACT clinical algorithms, and the Practical Approach to Care Kit (PACK). Clinical content is validated via a Rand/UCLA Appropriateness Method (RAM) process with expert panels in Zanzibar.


2. Pre-selected Treatment Order Sets
For diagnoses frequently associated with unnecessary or inappropriate antibiotic prescribing, the system presents pre-selected treatment order sets aligned with the Zanzibar Standard Treatment Guidelines and WHO AWaRe guidelines. This “positive default” design nudges clinicians toward evidence-based choices without restricting their autonomy.


3. Treatment Discordance Alerts
When a clinician selects an antibiotic for a diagnosis that does not require one, a pop-up alert flags the discordance. All ICD-10 diagnoses are categorised as: (A) antibiotic required, (B) antibiotic conditionally required, (C) antibiotic not required, or (D) antibiotic contraindicated.


4. AWaRe Classification of Antibiotics
All antibiotics in the system are classified according to the WHO AWaRe framework (Access, Watch, Reserve, Not Recommended):

Reserve and Not Recommended antibiotics appear in red
Watch antibiotics appear in orange
Access antibiotics appear in green

This visual coding reinforces appropriate antibiotic selection at the point of prescription.


5. Audit and Feedback Dashboards
Dashboards compare antibiotic prescription rates across participating health facilities, disaggregated by AWaRe category. These are shared with healthcare providers and relevant public health authorities to support facility-level reflection and improvement.

title: The Intervention
text:
Antibiotic Stewardship Club
The Antibiotic Stewardship Club is a structured monthly peer education intervention implemented in Arm 1 facilities only.
Each month, healthcare providers at the facility meet to:

Review their facility’s antibiotic prescribing patterns using the ZanEMR-AS dashboards
Discuss difficult or ambiguous cases
Identify local barriers to rational prescribing
Share strategies for communicating with patients who expect an antibiotic

The meetings are peer-led (not externally imposed), creating an accountable and supportive environment for behaviour change. Sessions run for approximately 1 hour and are facilitated by a trained clinical champion at each facility.

title: The Intervention
text:
Training and Mentorship
All facilities randomised to Arm 1 or Arm 2 receive:

Initial group training on the use of ZanEMR-AS and rational antibiotic prescribing principles
Supported mentorship visits by a clinical mentor during the intervention period, providing one-to-one or small-group guidance and addressing implementation challenges

Control facilities (Arm 3) continue to use standard ZanEMR with routine support from the Ministry of Health only.

title: Background
text:
Bacterial antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the most pressing public health threats of the 21st century. AMR arises when bacteria evolve mechanisms to resist the effects of antibiotics, rendering standard treatments ineffective and enabling infections to persist and spread.
Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing is a major driver of AMR.1 Antibiotics are prescribed when not needed, or the wrong antibiotic is chosen for the right indication, accelerating the selection of resistant bacterial strains.

title: Background
text:
Bacterial antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the most pressing public health threats of the 21st century. AMR arises when bacteria evolve mechanisms to resist the effects of antibiotics, rendering standard treatments ineffective and enabling infections to persist and spread.
Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing is a major driver of AMR.1 Antibiotics are prescribed when not needed, or the wrong antibiotic is chosen for the right indication, accelerating the selection of resistant bacterial strains.

title: Background
text:
The Burden in Primary Care
A systematic review found that antibiotics are prescribed in approximately 52% of primary care consultations in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) — a rate far exceeding clinical need.2
The consequences are stark: higher morbidity, higher mortality, longer illnesses, and increased healthcare costs, disproportionately affecting populations in LMICs who already face constrained access to health services.

title: Background
section: The Situation in Tanzania and Zanzibar
text:
The Situation in Tanzania and Zanzibar
Tanzania, including the semi-autonomous Zanzibar archipelago, faces a significant burden of AMR. Primary care dispensaries in Zanzibar serve as the first point of contact for the majority of the population and are a critical setting in which to improve antibiotic prescribing practices.
Several factors make rational antibiotic prescribing difficult in this context:

Clinical uncertainty: Many acute illnesses present with overlapping symptoms, making it hard to distinguish bacterial from viral infections without laboratory support.
Patient expectations: Patients often expect an antibiotic prescription, and clinicians may feel pressure to prescribe.
Longstanding prescribing culture: Widespread antibiotic prescribing has become normalized in many primary care settings.
Limited decision-support tools: Few digital tools are designed for managing adult and paediatric patients in sub-Saharan Africa, and those that exist are rarely integrated into the day-to-day clinical workflow.

title: Background
text:
The Opportunity
The Zanzibar Ministry of Health has developed and deployed the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record (ZanEMR) system across primary care dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands. This digital infrastructure creates a unique opportunity to embed antibiotic stewardship features directly into the clinician’s workflow — at the point of care, without duplicating data entry.
Digital clinical decision support systems (CDSS) integrated within electronic medical record (EMR) systems have shown potential to improve antibiotic stewardship, yet existing tools have important limitations: most target only children under five years of age, and many are not embedded in the health facilities’ digital infrastructure.
The DAWA project was designed to test whether combining this digital clinical decision support with structured training and peer education can meaningfully reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing at the primary care level in Zanzibar.

title: Study Design
text: The DAWA project is a master protocol comprising several interrelated studies designed to evaluate antibiotic stewardship interventions in primary care in Zanzibar, Tanzania.

title: Study Design
text: The DAWA project is a master protocol comprising several interrelated studies designed to evaluate antibiotic stewardship interventions in primary care in Zanzibar, Tanzania.

title: Study Design
section: DAWA I — Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial
text:
DAWA I — Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial
The primary study is a pragmatic three-arm parallel-group cluster randomized controlled trial (RCT) designed to evaluate the effectiveness of two intervention packages aimed at reducing antibiotic prescribing in primary care settings.
Study setting: Public dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar, Tanzania.
Cluster-level inclusion criteria:

Government or government-designated primary care health facilities (dispensaries) open to the general public.
Health facilities with the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record system (ZanEMR) installed in consultation rooms.

Patient-level inclusion criteria (primary analysis population):

Patients of all ages presenting for an acute infectious illness at months 4–6 after initiation of the interventions.

Interventions:







Arm
Intervention




Arm 1
ZanEMR-AS + initial group training + supported mentorship + monthly Antibiotic Stewardship Club meetings


Arm 2
ZanEMR-AS + initial group training + supported mentorship


Arm 3 (control)
Standard ZanEMR (usual care)



ZanEMR-AS refers to the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record system enhanced with antibiotic stewardship features, including:

Clinical decision support (pop-up guidance at point of care)
Pre-selected treatment order sets aligned with Zanzibar Standard Treatment Guidelines
Treatment discordance alerts
AWaRe classification of antibiotics (WHO framework)
Audit and feedback dashboards

Antibiotic Stewardship Club: Monthly peer-based education meetings at health facilities in Arm 1, where healthcare providers discuss rational antibiotic prescribing and review local prescribing patterns.

title: Study Design
text:
Primary Outcome
Proportion of encounters with an antibiotic prescribed, assessed at months 4–6 after initiation of the interventions.

title: Study Design
text:
Key Secondary Outcomes

Proportion of patients with worse clinical status at day 7 (harm outcome)
Proportion of patients with severe clinical outcome (hospitalization or death) by day 7
Proportion of encounters with an appropriate antibiotic prescribed
Proportion of antibiotics classified as “Access” according to WHO AWaRe
Patient-reported additional medication use by day 7

title: Study Design
text:
Sample Size
1,950 patients per arm (5,850 total).

title: Study Design
text:
Randomization
Randomization will be performed at the health facility (cluster) level with a 1:1:1 ratio, stratified by baseline antibiotic prescription proportion and health facility attendance rate.

title: Study Design
text:
Blinding
Research assistants enrolling patients and assessing day-7 outcomes will be blinded to allocation. Patients will not be informed of their facility’s allocation.

title: Study Design
text:
Estimated Timeline



Milestone
Date




Protocol finalization
2025–2026


Ethics approvals
2026


Estimated first enrolment
1 November 2026

title: Study Design
text:
Nested Studies
DAWA II — Long-term Outcome: Following completion of DAWA I, antibiotic prescribing will be assessed over the subsequent 6 months (months 6–12) after all active intervention activities have ceased, to evaluate sustainability of the effect.
Mixed-method studies: Semi-structured in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with healthcare providers and Ministry of Health stakeholders to assess acceptability and identify barriers and facilitators of rational antibiotic prescribing.
DAWA III — Staggered Rollout Evaluation: If ZanEMR-AS shows effectiveness in DAWA I, the antibiotic stewardship features will be rolled out to approximately 30–50 additional primary care facilities on Unguja and Pemba islands not included in the trial. DAWA III will evaluate whether the effect generalises to a broader, more heterogeneous set of facilities under real-world conditions — without stewardship clubs — using a prospective quasi-experimental staggered difference-in-differences (DiD) event study design. Successive rollout waves serve as natural experiments, with not-yet-treated facilities acting as controls for newly treated cohorts. The primary outcome is the monthly antibiotic prescribing proportion for acute illness. If rollout occurs too rapidly for meaningful staggering, the analysis reverts to an interrupted time series (ITS) approach.
Cost and cost-effectiveness study: Economic analysis of the interventions from patient, health facility, and public health system perspectives.

title: DAWA trial
text:
DAWA is a cluster-randomized trial evaluating the effectiveness of a digital clinical decision support system, training and mentorship to reduce antibiotic prescribing at primary care health facilities on Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar, Tanzania.


Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing is widespread in primary care settings in low- and middle-income countries, contributing to bacterial antimicrobial resistance (AMR), and excess morbidity and mortality.
Digital clinical decision support systems (CDSS) integrated within electronic medical record (EMR) systems have shown potential to improve antibiotic stewardship, yet existing tools have important limitations: most target only children under five years of age, and many are not embedded in the health facilities’ digital infrastructure.
The DAWA project tests a comprehensive antibiotic stewardship intervention — combining digital clinical decision support integrated within the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record (ZanEMR) system with targeted training and monthly peer education meetings — at primary care dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar.










Full title
A cluster-randomized trial to assess the effectiveness of a digital clinical decision support system, training and mentorship to reduce antibiotic prescribing at primary care health facilities in Tanzania


Short title
Digital Antibiotic stewardship and Training for Wahudumu wa Afya (DAWA)


Setting
Public dispensaries, Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar, Tanzania


Design
Three-arm parallel-group cluster randomized controlled trial


Sample size
5,850 patients (1,950 per arm)


Estimated first enrolment
1 November 2026


Registry
To be registered on ClinicalTrials.gov


Sponsor
Centre for Primary Care and Public Health (Unisanté), University of Lausanne, Switzerland






To evaluate the effectiveness of two antibiotic stewardship interventions — both incorporating clinical decision support integrated within an electronic medical record system, and one with an additional monthly peer education component — to reduce antibiotic prescribing for acute infections at primary care health facilities on Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar, Tanzania.




Arm 1 (Multifaceted Intervention): ZanEMR-AS (digital clinical decision support integrated within ZanEMR) with initial group training, supported mentorship visits, and monthly Antibiotic Stewardship Club meetings.
Arm 2 (Digital Support Alone): ZanEMR-AS with initial group training and supported mentorship visits.
Arm 3 (Usual Care): Standard ZanEMR (no antibiotic stewardship features).


The DAWA project is a collaboration between the Centre for Primary Care and Public Health (Unisanté), University of Lausanne; the Ifakara Health Institute (IHI), Tanzania; and the Zanzibar Ministry of Health. It is funded by the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF).





The DAWA trial takes place at public dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands, Zanzibar, an archipelago off the east coast of Tanzania.






Zanzibar





Tanzania
